attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following changes occurs in the sinoatrial node as a result of stimulation of beta-1 adrenoreceptors?
options: Decreased calcium influx through L-type calcium channels
Increased sodium efflux through HCN channels
Increased potassium efflux through ATP-sensitive potassium channels
Decrease in the slope of spontaneous depolarisation
attributes.explanation:
This question tests understanding of the physiological effects of beta-1 adrenoreceptor stimulation on the sinoatrial (SA) node.

Increased cAMP production is the correct answer. When beta-1 adrenoreceptors in the SA node are stimulated (e.g., by sympathetic nervous system activation or circulating catecholamines), they activate adenylyl cyclase via G-proteins. This leads to increased production of cyclic AMP (cAMP), which is a crucial second messenger in the signalling cascade.

Decreased calcium influx through L-type calcium channels is incorrect. Beta-1 stimulation actually increases calcium influx through L-type calcium channels. This is mediated by cAMP-dependent protein kinase A (PKA), which phosphorylates these channels, increasing their open probability and conductance.

Increased sodium efflux through HCN channels is also incorrect. The hyperpolarisation-activated cyclic nucleotide-gated (HCN) channels, responsible for the 'funny current' (If), actually mediate sodium influx, not efflux. Moreover, beta-1 stimulation increases this inward current.

Increased potassium efflux through ATP-sensitive potassium channels is not a direct effect of beta-1 stimulation. While these channels play a role in cellular metabolism and excitability, they are not primary targets of beta-1 adrenoreceptor signalling in the context of heart rate regulation.

Decrease in the slope of spontaneous depolarisation is incorrect. The combined effects of increased calcium influx through L-type calcium channels and increased sodium influx through HCN channels result in a steeper slope of spontaneous depolarisation, not a decrease.

The overall effect of these changes is an increase in the firing rate of the SA node, leading to an increased heart rate, as seen in this patient with sinus tachycardia. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for comprehending how various medications (e.g., beta-blockers) and pathological conditions affect heart rate.
learningPoints: Stimulation of beta-1 adrenoreceptors in the sinoatrial node increases cAMP production, leading to increased heart rate.
Increased cAMP enhances calcium influx through L-type calcium channels, contributing to faster spontaneous depolarisation.
The 'funny current' (If) through HCN channels is also augmented by beta-1 stimulation, further increasing heart rate.
references: Santos IN, Spadari-Bratfisch RC. Stress and cardiac beta adrenoceptors. Stress. 2006 Jun;9(2):69-84. doi: 10.1080/10253890600771858. PMID: 16895831.
Tortora, G. J., Derrickson, B., Burkett, B., Cooke, J., Di Pietro, F., Diversi, T., ... & Summers, S.. Principles of Anatomy and Physiology, 3rd Asia-Pacific Edition. 2022. John Wiley & Sons

Kim MS, Monfredi O, Maltseva LA, Lakatta EG, Maltsev VA. β-Adrenergic Stimulation Synchronizes a Broad Spectrum of Action Potential Firing Rates of Cardiac Pacemaker Cells toward a Higher Population Average. Cells. 2021 Aug 18;10(8):2124. doi: 10.3390/cells10082124. PMID: 34440893; PMCID: PMC8391682.

MacDonald EA, Rose RA, Quinn TA. Neurohumoral Control of Sinoatrial Node Activity and Heart Rate: Insight From Experimental Models and Findings From Humans. Front Physiol. 2020 Mar 3;11:170. doi: 10.3389/fphys.2020.00170. PMID: 32194439; PMCID: PMC7063087.
